content.inputFormat: First line contains T, the number of test cases. Each test case contains two lines: the first line contains an integer N, the length of the array, and the second line contains N space-separated integers A1 … AN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print the minimal possible length of the original array on a new line.

sampleTestCases.explanation: First array contains only one distinct value, so the minimal original array length is 1. The second array has distinct values {1,2,3}; starting from [1,2,3] we can copy the prefix [1,2] and paste it at the end to obtain the final array.
All three values are different, therefore the initial array must contain all three.
Four distinct numbers require an initial array of length four.

solution.approach: The problem can be solved by finding the number of distinct elements in the array. This is because the 'Copy-Paste' operation does not introduce any new distinct elements. We can use a set data structure to store the distinct elements and then return the size of the set as the minimal possible length of the original array.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, O(T*N) total. The set operations (insertion and lookup) take constant time on average.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) — the dominant structure is the set used to store the distinct elements.

code.Python3:
import sys

def solve():
    data = sys.stdin.buffer.read().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    out = []
    for _ in range(T):
        N = int(next(it))
        s = set()
        for _ in range(N):
            s.add(int(next(it)))
        out.append(str(len(s)))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
